qa_section_text:
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
Use innovative approaches
Improve overall quality of decisions
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
The Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP) administers a compliance
Regime. The compliance regime inspects employers who use the Program to
verify their compliance with the program conditions of the Immigration and
Refugee Protection Regulations (IRPR). The Program needs a system that can
help generate a random list of employers to be inspected. In the past, these
lists were made using purely random sampling methods, and more recently, a
predictive model which took into consideration simple risk factors in the
generation of random samples was used.
The new approach leverages additional data sources that can be used to flag
new risk factors in accordance with compliance with the IRPR in the Labour
Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) and informed by observations noted by
inspectors. The new approach is to account for employers who are at higher
risk of potential non-compliance with program requirements and to improve
the reach of inspections. In addition, this new system will also contribute to
the quality of the compliance regime by enabling improved data-informed
inspections.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
The TFWP compliance regime, including inspections, helps to maintain
program integrity and to help protect temporary foreign workers rights, by
holding employers accountable for any violation of program requirements.
Through the inspections process, employer’s adherence to program
conditions is verified, and administrative monetary penalties and/or bans from
the program are issued to non-compliant employers. There are approximately
50,000 employers who are approved to use the TFWP, and only a percentage
are selected for inspections, in any given year. Implementing this system
would allow the Program to prioritize and select employers with the risk
factors linked to potential non-compliance.
4. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
The automation will make it possible to both use a significant amount of data
and will include the ability to quickly respond to changes in the needs of the
TFWP. It will allow ongoing support of the needs of the TFWP, whether it be
changes to risk flags or data repositories, or changes to the quantity and
distribution of cases.
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
The project consists of two case selection activities – random selection
among high-risk employers, and random selection from the entire population
of employers to serve as a baseline. There is also a process for modifying the
parameters and data used as needed (dependent on internal consultations,
monitoring, and data quality evaluation). The system is limited to selecting
employers and referring cases to the case management system. All activities
after this selection will be carried out by human staff following the regular
operational procedures as set out in the regulations and internal guidance.
This includes human autonomy, leaving the decision making to the delegated
decision maker.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
None identified.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
Purely random sampling was considered. Random sampling will still be used
for a percentage of employers selected for inspection, but the automated
process, which will provide random selections among employers deemed
"high-risk" will be primarily used. Incorporating risk of program misuse into
employer selection is necessary for TFWP compliance to mitigate these risks.
One of the goals of the automated process is to use all available data at its
disposal and use automation to help connect data to increase focus of
inspections on employers at highest risk of potential program misuse, and
help inspectors detect and address employers' non-compliance more
effectively. The automated process will allow TFWP to identify cases that are
both more timely and relevant compared to those obtained through purely
random selection.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service qua
_full_text:
Algorithmic Impact Assessment Results
Version: 0.10.0
Project Details
1. Job Title
Business Analysts and Data Scientists
2. Department
Employment and Social Development (Department of)
3. Branch
Integrity Services Branch and Temporary Foreign Worker Branch
4. Project Title
Temporary Foreign Worker Program Case Selection Algorithm
5. Departmental Program (from Department Results Framework)
Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP)
6. Project Phase
Implementation [ Points: 0 ]
7. Please provide a project description:
A set of algorithms and business rules to prioritize TFWP cases for inspection
based on the existence and severity of established risk factors.
About The System
8. Please check which of the following capabilities apply to your system.
Risk assessment: Analyzing very large data sets to identify patterns and
recommend courses of action and in some cases trigger specific actions
Process optimization and workflow automation: Analyzing large data sets to
identify and anomalies, cluster patterns, predict outcomes or ways to
optimize; and automate specific workflows
Section 1: Impact Level : 1
Current Score: 30
Raw Impact Score: 30
Mitigation Score: 22
Section 2: Requirements Specific to Impact Level 1
Peer review
None
Gender-based Analysis Plus
None
Notice
None
Human-in-the-loop for decisions
Decisions may be rendered without direct human involvement.
Explanation
In addition to any applicable legal requirement, ensure that a meaningful explanation is published
for common decision results. The explanation must provide a general description of:
the role of the system in the decision-making process;
input data, its source and method of collection;
the criteria used to evaluate input data and the operations applied to process it;
the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to interpret it in the
context of the administrative decision; and
the principal factors behind a decision.
Explanations must also inform clients of relevant recourse options, where appropriate.
Descriptions must be made available in plain language through the Algorithmic Impact
Assessment and discoverable via a departmental website.
Training
None
IT and business continuity management
None
Approval for the system to operate
None
Other requirements
The Directive on Automated Decision-Making also includes other requirements that must be met
for all impact levels.
Directive on Automated Decision-Making
Contact your institution's ATIP office to discuss the requirement for a Privacy Impact
Assessment as per the Directive on Privacy Impact Assessment.
Section 3: Questions and Answers
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
Use innovative approaches
Improve overall quality of decisions
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
The Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP) administers a compliance
Regime. The compliance regime inspects employers who use the Program to
verify their compliance with the program conditions of the Immigration and
Refugee Protection Regulations (IRPR). The Program needs a system that can
help generate a random list of employers to be inspected. In the past, these
lists were made using purely random sampling methods, and more recently, a
predictive model which took into consideration simple risk factors in the
generation of random samples was used.
The new approach leverages additional data sources that can be used to flag
new risk factors in accordance with compliance with the IRPR in the Labour
Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) and informed by observations noted by
inspectors. The new approach is to account for employers who are at higher
risk of potential non-compliance with program requirements and to improve
the reach of inspections. In addition, this new system will also contribute to
the quality of the compliance regime by enabling improved data-informed
inspections.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
The TFWP compliance regime, including inspections, helps to maintain
program integrity and to help protect temporary foreign workers rights, by
holding employers accountable for any violation of program requirements.
Through the inspections process, employer’s adherence to program
conditions is verified, and administrative monetary penalties and/or bans from
the program are issued to non-compliant employers. There are approximately
50,000 employers who are approved to use the TFWP, and only a percentage
are selected for inspections, in any given year. Implementing this system
would allow the Program to prioritize and select employers with the risk
factors linked to potential non-compliance.
4. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
The automation will make it possible to both use a significant amount of data
and will include the ability to quickly respond to changes in the needs of the
TFWP. It will allow ongoing support of the needs of the TFWP, whether it be
changes to risk flags or data repositories, or changes to the quantity and
distribution of cases.
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
The project consists of two case selection activities – random selection
among high-risk employers, and random selection from the entire population
of employers to serve as a baseline. There is also a process for modifying the
parameters and data used as needed (dependent on internal consultations,
monitoring, and data quality evaluation). The system is limited to selecting
employers and referring cases to the case management system. All activities
after this selection will be carried out by human staff following the regular
operational procedures as set out in the regulations and internal guidance.
This includes human autonomy, leaving the decision making to the delegated
decision maker.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
None identified.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
Purely random sampling was considered. Random sampling will still be used
for a percentage of employers selected for inspection, but the automated
process, which will provide random selections among employers deemed
"high-risk" will be primarily used. Incorporating risk of program misuse into
employer selection is necessary for TFWP compliance to mitigate these risks.
One of the goals of the automated process is to use all available data at its
disposal and use automation to help connect data to increase focus of
inspections on employers at highest risk of potential program misuse, and
help inspectors detect and address employers' non-compliance more
effectively. The automated process will allow TFWP to identify cases that are
both more timely and relevant compared to those obtained through purely
random selection.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service quality is not as high
Other (please specify)
11. Please describe
Given the TFWP's compliance regime is continuously evolving, it would be a
missed opportunity not to explore this type of technology. As a result of not
deploying the system, there could be serious integrity issues in the TFWP that
could go unaddressed, and operational resources could be directed to inspect
fewer cases of potential program misuse.
Risk Profile
12. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (e.g. because of privacy concerns)
and/or frequent litigation?
Yes [ Points: +3 ]
13. Are clients in this line of business particularly vulnerable?
No [ Points: +0 ]
14. Are stakes of the decisions very high?
No [ Points: +0 ]
15. Will this project have major impacts on staff, either in terms of their numbers or their roles?
No [ Points: +0 ]
16. Will the use of the system create or exacerbate barriers for persons with disabilities?
No [ Points: +0 ]
Project Authority
17. Will you require new policy authority for this project?
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Algorithm
18. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
No [ Points: +0 ]
19. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Decision
20. Please describe the decision(s) that will be automated.
The process will select employers who use the TFWP for inspection by
Service Canada. The number of selections by region and stream (of the
TFWP) will be determined by the TFWP Branch. Cases will be referred to the
case management system, where human staff will take full control of
managing, distributing, and carrying out cases according to relevant
procedures, regulations, and guidance.
21. Does the decision pertain to any of the categories below (check all that apply):
Other (please specify) [ Points: +1 ]
22. Please describe
Employers who use the TFW Program can be randomly selected for an
inspection; selected based on tips or allegations of non-compliance; or based
on past non-compliance with program requirements. If an employer fails to
meet program requirements and/or conditions, consequences can include
administrative monetary penalties and a temporary or permanent ban from
the Program.
Impact Assessment
23. Which of the following best describes the type of automation you are planning?
Partial automation (the system will contribute to administrative decision-
making by supporting an officer through assessments, recommendations,
intermediate decisions, or other outputs) [ Points: +2 ]
24. Please describe the role of the system in the decision-making process.
The system will support the decision-making process by generating a list of
employers to be considered for inspection. As previously stated, the system
will make random selections from among potentially "high-risk" employers
and will also make a percentage of purely random selections from the entire
employer population. The system will make no recommendations regarding
the case to the human staff; from the point of referral a human will be
responsible for all decisions made in the case. Some details of the specific
risk flag the employer triggered may be communicated to staff (via the case
management system), and if necessary and authorized, data will be provided.
At all times the investigators will follow the established inspection
procedures, regulations, and guidance, in accordance with their training to
conduct compliance inspections on employers.
25. Will the system be making decisions or assessments that require judgement or discretion?
No [ Points: +0 ]
26. Please describe the criteria used to evaluate client data and the operations applied to
process it.
Disclosure of this criteria is restricted due to the nature of the solution.
27. Please describe the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to
interpret it in the context of the administrative decision.
The system will produce a list of business numbers (BNs) and associated
LMIA data that will be referred to the case management system for the
creation of inspection cases. The output will consist of data in the same
format used by other cases which are manually loaded into the case
management system.
28. Will the system perform an assessment or other operation that would not otherwise be
completed by a human?
No [ Points: +0 ]
29. Is the system used by a different part of the organization than the ones who developed it?
No [ Points: +0 ]
30. Are the impacts resulting from the decision reversible?
Reversible [ Points: +1 ]
31. How long will impacts from the decision last?
Impacts are most likely to be brief [ Points: +1 ]
32. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
Upon referral to the case management system, the distribution, assignment,
and initiation of an inspection will be carried out by human staff. They will
maintain the autonomy to remove or delete cases, and follow all applicable
procedures, regulations, and guidance. The system will make no
recommendations regarding decisions in the inspection.
33. The impacts that the decision will have on the rights or freedoms of individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
34. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The IRPR and LMIA agreement state that any employer can be inspected
under the random selection trigger. All employers using the TFWP may be
subject to random selection for an inspection.
35. The impacts that the decision will have on the equality, dignity, privacy, and autonomy of
individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
36. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
All employers may be subject to inspection at any time, and inspections will
follow the existing established guidelines and procedures.
37. The impacts that the decision will have on the health and well-being of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
38. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
By selecting employers with more potential risk of non-compliance with IRPR,
the automation may have a positive impact on the health and safety and
security of temporary foreign workers, who work for participating employers.
It is hoped that increased focus of inspections on these high-risk employers
will lead to greater program compliance.
39. The impacts that the decision will have on the economic interests of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
40. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The outcome of the system will have no effect on employers using the
program. The outcome of the inspection may have a negative financial impact
on employers who are misusing the Program, as they may be fined or banned
from the program according to the IRPR.
41. The impacts that the decision will have on the ongoing sustainability of an environmental
ecosystem, will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
42. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The automation will not impact the number of inspections initiated by the
program, so operational activities such as travel will not be affected.
About the Data - A. Data Source
43. Will the Automated Decision System use personal information as input data?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
44. Have you verified that the use of personal information is limited to only what is directly
related to delivering a program or service?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
45. Is the personal information of individuals being used in a decision-making process that
directly affects those individuals?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
46. Have you verified if the system is using personal information in a way that is consistent
with: (a) the current Personal Information Banks (PIBs) and Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs)
of your programs or (b) planned or implemented modifications to the PIBs or PIAs that take new
uses and processes into account?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
47. Please list relevant PIB Bank Numbers.
PPU 040; PPU 715
48. What is the highest security classification of the input data used by the system? (Select one)
Protected B / Protected C [ Points: +3 ]
49. Who controls the data?
Federal government [ Points: +1 ]
50. Will the system use data from multiple different sources?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
51. Will the system require input data from an Internet- or telephony-connected device? (e.g.
Internet of Things, sensor)
No [ Points: +0 ]
52. Will the system interface with other IT systems?
No [ Points: +0 ]
53. Who collected the data used for training the system?
Your institution [ Points: +1 ]
54. Who collected the input data used by the system?
Another federal institution [ Points: +2 ]
55. Please describe the input data collected and used by the system, its source, and method of
collection.
The data collected comes from various Federal Departments and Provincial
governments. The TFWP is a jointly administered program between IRCC,
CBSA, and ESDC, with inspections conducted by Service Canada. Data
primarily comes from those sources. In addition, there are other departmental
data (ESDC) in use under DESDA, and Information Sharing Agreements (ISAs)
with various Provincial jurisdictions for the purposes of TFWP Integrity.
About the Data - B. Type of Data
56. Will the system require the analysis of unstructured data to render a recommendation or a
decision?
No [ Points: 0 ]
Section 3.2: Mitigation Questions and Answers
Consultations
1. Internal Stakeholders (federal institutions, including the federal public service)
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
2. Which Internal Stakeholders have you engaged?
Strategic Policy and Planning
Data Governance
Program Policy
Legal Services
Access to Information and Privacy Office
3. External Stakeholders (groups in other sectors or jurisdictions)
No [ Points: +0 ]
De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Data Quality
4. Do you have documented processes in place to test datasets against biases and other
unexpected outcomes? This could include experience in applying frameworks, methods,
guidelines or other assessment tools.
No [ Points: +0 ]
5. Is this information publicly available?
No [ Points: +0 ]
6. Have you developed a process to document how data quality issues were resolved during the
design process?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
7. Is this information publicly available?
No [ Points: +0 ]
8. Have you undertaken a Gender Based Analysis Plus of the data?
No [ Points: +0 ]
9. Is this information publicly available?
No [ Points: +0 ]
10. Have you assigned accountability in your institution for the design, development,
maintenance, and improvement of the system?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
11. Do you have a documented process to manage the risk that outdated or unreliable data is
used to make an automated decision?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
12. Is this information publicly available?
No [ Points: +0 ]
13. Is the data used for this system posted on the Open Government Portal?
No [ Points: +0 ]
De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Procedural
Fairness
14. Does the audit trail identify the authority or delegated authority identified in legislation?
No [ Points: +0 ]
15. Does the system provide an audit trail that records all the recommendations or decisions
made by the system?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
16. Are all key decision points identifiable in the audit trail?
No [ Points: +0 ]
17. Are all key decision points within the automated system's logic linked to the relevant
legislation, policy or procedures?
No [ Points: +0 ]
18. Do you maintain a current and up to date log detailing all of the changes made to the model
and the system?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
19. Does the system's audit trail indicate all of the decision points made by the system?
No [ Points: +0 ]
20. Can the audit trail generated by the system be used to help generate a notification of the
decision (including a statement of reasons or other notifications) where required?
No [ Points: +0 ]
21. Does the audit trail identify precisely which version of the system was used for each
decision it supports?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
22. Does the audit trail show who an authorized decision-maker is?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
23. Is the system able to produce reasons for its decisions or recommendations when required?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
24. Is there a process in place to grant, monitor, and revoke access permission to the system?
No [ Points: +0 ]
25. Is there a mechanism to capture feedback by users of the system?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
26. Is there a recourse process established for clients that wish to challenge the decision?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
27. Does the system enable human override of system decisions?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
28. Is there a process in place to log the instances when overrides were performed?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
29. Does the system's audit trail include change control processes to record modifications to the
system's operation or performance?
No [ Points: +0 ]
30. Have you prepared a concept case to the Government of Canada Enterprise Architecture
Review Board?
No [ Points: +0 ]
De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Privacy
31. If your system uses or creates personal information, have you undertaken a Privacy Impact
Assessment, or updated an existing one?
No [ Points: +0 ]
32. Have you undertaken other types of privacy assessments for your automation project?
Please describe any relevant efforts.
A formal privacy risk assessment was conducted by ESDC’s Privacy
Management Division in accordance with established privacy principles, TBS
policies and directives, and the Privacy Act and Part 4 of the DESDA, to ensure
that privacy controls and safeguards were well-established, and privacy risks
subject to an actionable plan for mitigation.
33. Have you designed and built security and privacy into your systems from the concept stage
of the project?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
34. Is the information used within a closed system (i.e. no connections to the Internet, Intranet
or any other system)?
No [ Points: +0 ]
35. If the sharing of personal information is involved, has an agreement or arrangement with
appropriate safeguards been established?
No [ Points: +0 ]
36. Will you de-identify any personal information used or created by the system at any point in
the lifecycle?
No [ Points: +0 ]
